danger_signals.meaning: Secondary threats spawned by main antagonist; escalating danger

danger_signals.meaning: Hostile foreign realm; departure from safety of original world

safety_signals.meaning: Mission objective fulfilled; safety restored to innocent

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Second World dimension; The Wasteland stage; The Lab stage; Yasko Mines stage

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Shaq as lone protagonist vs. multiple enemy fighters
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Original Tokyo dojo; basketball game venue; journey stages between worlds

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tokyo dojo setting; basketball game environment; realistic contemporary martial arts academy
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shaq engaged in charity/athletic activities; social normalcy; NPC mentorship

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition to Second World; altered environment aesthetics; otherworldly stage design (Wasteland, Lab, Mines)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Forced combat engagement; sequential enemy encounters; mandatory progression through hostile territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mummy character dominance; undead/cursed visual motifs; hostile minion presence throughout stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy AI aggression; stage hazards; multi-round combat scenarios; minion spawning

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Beast character presence in original world basketball game
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy infiltration of safety zone; rematch scenario; threat persistence despite primary objective completion

environmental_storytelling.example: Multiple stages (North Gate/South Gate, The Lab, The Wasteland, Yasko Mines) communicate dimensional geography and danger escalation through environment variety
Genesis version includes 5 additional playable characters (Auroch, Colonel, Diesel, Leotsu, Nezu) vs. SNES version - roster size/platform differences communicate expanded narrative scope

environmental_storytelling.technique: Sprite size and animation as visual language
environmental_storytelling.example: Digitized graphics with intentionally small sprite size balanced by fast game speed - conveys arcade action intensity and efficiency of combat
Concealed finishing moves unlocked via button sequence - parallels Mortal Kombat tension mechanic; hidden danger state activation mirrors secret threat exposure

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Involuntary dimensional transport; forced hero role assumption; combat engagement with escalating enemy types
Initial NPC interaction; knowledge conferral; legitimacy granting for warrior status
Multi-stage boss progression; minion command; dimensional imprisonment of innocents
Initial defeat in Second World; world-crossing infiltration; rematch initiation in original basketball context
Passivity requiring protection; objective marker; success indicator upon rescue

notes: Shaq Fu operates as a narrative-light fighting game where world-state communication relies on stage/environment design, character model aesthetics (mummy vs. martial artist), and narrative context (dojo -> dimensional transport -> evil dimension -> contaminated return). The game lacks complex political faction systems; instead uses binary good/evil opposition with Sett-Ra's supernatural evil contrasted against basketball-mundane-world safety. Platform variations (Genesis extra 5 characters, 3 extra stages vs. SNES reduced roster) communicate world-building scope differently across hardware. The hidden blood code mirrors danger-state escalation mechanics without explicit visual/narrative integration. Minimal documented visual communication of political complexity; primarily operates on archetypal hero-vs-evil framework with dimensional imprisonment as primary world-state mechanism.
sources_used: wikipedia - Shaq Fu article (plot, development, ports, character/stage listings)
